title: The State of the Art in Visualization Literacy
authors: Matthew Varona, Karen Bonilla, Maryam Hedayati, Alark Joshi, Matthew Kay, Lane Harrison, Carolina Nobre
summary: We systematically review 374 papers to develop frameworks for studying visualization literacy and highlight the field's most pressing challenges.
bibtex:
@misc{VaronaetalStateArtVisualization2025a,
  title = {The {{State}} of the {{Art}} in {{Visualization Literacy}}},
  author = {Varona, Matthew and Bonilla, Karen and Hedayati, Maryam and Joshi, Alark and Kay, Matthew and Harrison, Lane and Nobre, Carolina},
  year = 2025,
  month = aug,
  number = {arXiv:2509.01018},
  eprint = {2509.01018},
  primaryclass = {cs},
  publisher = {arXiv},
  doi = {10.48550/arXiv.2509.01018},
  urldate = {2025-09-08},
  abstract = {Research in visualization literacy explores the skills required to engage with visualizations. This state-of-the-art report surveys the current literature in visualization literacy to provide a comprehensive overview of the field. We propose a taxonomy of visualization literacy that organizes the field into competency themes and research categories. To address ambiguity surrounding the term 'visualization literacy', we provide a framework for operationalizing visualization literacy based on application contexts (including domain, scenario, and audience) and relevant competencies, which are categorized under consumption, construction, critique, and connection. Research contributions are organized into five categories: ontology, assessment, mechanisms, populiteracy, and intervention. For each category, we identify key trends, discuss which competencies are addressed, highlight open challenges, and examine how advancements within these areas inform and reinforce each other, driving progress in the field.},
  archiveprefix = {arXiv},
  howpublished = {\url{http://arxiv.org/abs/2509.01018}},
  keywords = {Computer Science - Human-Computer Interaction}
}

title: Theory is Shapes
authors: Matthew Varona, Maryam Hedayati, Matthew Kay, Carolina Nobre
summary: A satirical treatise on how shapes shape theory. Winner of the 'Tastiest' award at the alt.vis 2025 workshop.
bibtex:
@misc{VaronaetalTheoryShapes2025,
  title = {Theory Is {{Shapes}}},
  author = {Varona, Matthew and Hedayati, Maryam and Kay, Matthew and Nobre, Carolina},
  year = 2025,
  month = oct,
  number = {arXiv:2510.01382},
  eprint = {2510.01382},
  primaryclass = {cs},
  publisher = {arXiv},
  doi = {10.48550/arXiv.2510.01382},
  urldate = {2025-11-29},
  abstract = {'Theory figures' are a staple of theoretical visualization research. Common shapes such as Cartesian planes and flowcharts can be used not only to explain conceptual contributions, but to think through and refine the contribution itself. Yet, theory figures tend to be limited to a set of standard shapes, limiting the creative and expressive potential of visualization theory. In this work, we explore how the shapes used in theory figures afford different understandings and explanations of their underlying phenomena. We speculate on the value of visualizing theories using more expressive configurations, such as icebergs, horseshoes, Mobius strips, and BLT sandwiches. By reflecting on figure-making's generative role in the practice of theorizing, we conclude that theory is, in fact, shapes.},
  archiveprefix = {arXiv},
  howpublished = {\url{http://arxiv.org/abs/2510.01382}},
  keywords = {Computer Science - Human-Computer Interaction}
}

title: Negotiating Relationships with ChatGPT: Perceptions, External Influences, and Strategies for AI Companionship
authors: Patrick Yung Kang Lee*, Jessica Bo*, Zixin Zhao*, Paula Akemi Aoyagui*, Matthew Varona*, Ashton Anderson, Anastasia Kuzminykh, Fanny Chevalier, Carolina Nobre
summary: Combining interviews, surveys, and large-scale Reddit analysis to explore relationship dynamics between humans and their AI romantic companions.
bibtex:
@misc{LeeetalNegotiatingRelationshipsChatGPT2026,
  title = {Negotiating {{Relationships}} with {{ChatGPT}}: {{Perceptions}}, {{External Influences}}, and {{Strategies}} for {{AI Companionship}}},
  shorttitle = {Negotiating {{Relationships}} with {{ChatGPT}}},
  author = {Lee, Patrick Yung Kang and Bo, Jessica Y. and Zhao, Zixin and Aoyagui, Paula Akemi and Varona, Matthew and Anderson, Ashton and Kuzminykh, Anastasia and Chevalier, Fanny and Nobre, Carolina},
  year = 2026,
  month = jan,
  number = {arXiv:2601.13188},
  eprint = {2601.13188},
  primaryclass = {cs},
  publisher = {arXiv},
  doi = {10.48550/arXiv.2601.13188},
  urldate = {2026-03-06},
  abstract = {Individuals are turning to increasingly anthropomorphic, general-purpose chatbots for AI companionship, rather than roleplay-specific platforms. However, not much is known about how individuals perceive and conduct their relationships with general-purpose chatbots. We analyzed semi-structured interviews (n=13), survey responses (n=43), and community discussions on Reddit (41k+ posts and comments) to triangulate the internal dynamics, external influences, and steering strategies that shape AI companion relationships. We learned that individuals conceptualize their companions based on an interplay of their beliefs about the companion's own agency and the autonomy permitted by the platform, how they pursue interactions with the companion, and the perceived initiatives that the companion takes. In combination with the external entities that affect relationship dynamics, particularly model updates that can derail companion behaviour and stability, individuals make use of different types of steering strategies to preserve their relationship, for example, by setting behavioural instructions or porting to other AI platforms. We discuss implications for accountability and transparency in AI systems, where emotional connection competes with broader product objectives and safety constraints.},
  archiveprefix = {arXiv},
  howpublished = {\url{http://arxiv.org/abs/2601.13188}},
  keywords = {Computer Science - Computers and Society,Computer Science - Human-Computer Interaction}
}
